**Pre-Lent** begins the [Christian](/wiki/Christian "Christian") time of preparation for [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), in the three weeks before [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[1]](#cite_note-Taft-1) This period launches a campaign of [catechesis](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis"), reflected in the [liturgical](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") readings.[[2]](#cite_note-Chavesse-2) Its best-known feature is its concluding three-day festival, [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival") or [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Western Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pre-Lent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Western Churches")]

The pre-Lenten period begins with [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima"), first documented in [Gregory the Great](/wiki/Gregory_the_Great "Gregory the Great").[[4]](#cite_note-Crampton-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) It traditionally opens a period of religious instruction leading to the reception of [catechumens](/wiki/Catechumens "Catechumens") at Easter,[[2]](#cite_note-Chavesse-2) supported by events such as [mystery plays](/wiki/Mystery_plays "Mystery plays").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The traditional [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary") for the [canonical hours](/wiki/Canonical_hours "Canonical hours") summarizes [salvation history](/wiki/Salvation_history "Salvation history") between Septuagesima and Easter, beginning with a reading of the [Book of Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis").[[7]](#cite_note-7) The last three days of pre-Lent are known as [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival"), [Shrovetide](/wiki/Shrovetide "Shrovetide"), or [Fastelavn](/wiki/Fastelavn "Fastelavn"), a festival ending with [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") or [Mardi Gras](/wiki/Mardi_Gras "Mardi Gras").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") of the period is characterized by violet [vestments](/wiki/Vestments "Vestments") (except on feasts) and a more penitential mood.[[10]](#cite_note-Mahrt-10) From Septuagesima, [Alleluia](/wiki/Alleluia "Alleluia") is not traditionally sung in worship.[[10]](#cite_note-Mahrt-10) A sermon of [Hildebert](/wiki/Hildebert "Hildebert") explains the logic of this practice:

> Moreover this day, which is, as it were, the gate of the fast, and takes away from us the song of joy, that is, Alleluia, shows our state of penitence and sorrow, teaching us that we ought to cease from immoderate joy, and remain in tears of repentance. We therefore repeat the hymn again and again, and address Alleluia itself, desirous of retaining it as a guest, and saying to it, Abide with us, for the day is far spent; and we then give it our last farewell, saying, The good angel of the Lord accompany you, that you may return again to us; that we may know that we shall not have perfect joy, until, renewed by the Body and Blood of our Redeemer, we receive that song with gladness.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

A custom developed during the Middle Ages of children burying an Alleluia on Septuagesima Eve.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It is possible for [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas") (2 February) to fall after Septuagesima Sunday, creating occasional musical challenges.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The pre-Lenten period includes three Sundays, whose names refer to the approximate periods of seventy, sixty, and fifty days before Easter. The Sundays are also known by the opening word of the [introit](/wiki/Introit "Introit") for the day:[[14]](#cite_note-14)

* [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima") or *Circumdederunt* (from *Circumdederunt me gemitus mortis*, [Psalm 18:5](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Psalms%2018:5&version=nrsv)), centred on the [Parable of the Workers in the Vineyard](/wiki/Parable_of_the_Workers_in_the_Vineyard "Parable of the Workers in the Vineyard") ([Matthew 20:1–16](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%2020:1–16&version=nrsv)),[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) already used for this Sunday in the time of Gregory the Great[[4]](#cite_note-Crampton-4)
* [Sexagesima](/wiki/Sexagesima "Sexagesima") or *Exsurge* (from *Exsurge quare obdormis Domine*, [Psalm 44:24](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Psalms%2044:24&version=nrsv)), featuring the [Parable of the Sower](/wiki/Parable_of_the_Sower "Parable of the Sower") ([Luke 8:4–15](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%208:4–15&version=nrsv))[[17]](#cite_note-17)
* [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima") or *Estomihi* (from *Esto mihi in Deum protectorem*, [Psalm 31:3](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Psalms%2031:3&version=nrsv)), with the story of Jesus [healing the blind near Jericho](/wiki/Healing_the_blind_near_Jericho "Healing the blind near Jericho") ([Luke 18:31–43](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%2018:31–43&version=nrsv))[[18]](#cite_note-18)

While [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") liturgies continue to mark a pre-Lenten period, the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") after 1970 eliminated Septuagesima, Sexagesima, and Quinquagesima.[[19]](#cite_note-Pristas-19) The [preconciliar rites after the Second Vatican Council](/wiki/Preconciliar_rites_after_the_Second_Vatican_Council "Preconciliar rites after the Second Vatican Council") also continue to mark these seventeen days. The [Revised Common Lectionary](/wiki/Revised_Common_Lectionary "Revised Common Lectionary") does not associate particular readings with the Sundays before Lent, but some users of this lectionary, such as the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), have retrofitted a pre-Lenten provision.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Eastern Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pre-Lent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eastern Churches")]

Main articles: [Paschal cycle](/wiki/Paschal_cycle "Paschal cycle") and [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")

In the [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") [Catholic](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") churches, the pre-Lenten period lasts three weeks.[[1]](#cite_note-Taft-1) It begins on the Sunday of the [Publican and the Pharisee](/wiki/Pharisee_and_the_Publican "Pharisee and the Publican") and continues through the [Sunday of Forgiveness](/wiki/Sunday_of_Forgiveness "Sunday of Forgiveness"), the day before the beginning of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent").[[21]](#cite_note-Pfatteicher-21) Since the liturgical day begins at sunset, and Great Lent begins on a Monday, the point at which Great Lent begins is at [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") on the night of the Sunday of Forgiveness, with a feast of mutual forgiveness. In some [monasteries](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery"), this ceremony is performed at [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline") instead of Vespers.

Thus begins the first day of the Great Fast, which is known as [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"). The weeks of pre-Lent and Great Lent are anticipatory and preparatory by nature. They begin on Monday and end on Sunday, each week being named for the theme of the upcoming Sunday. The hymns used during the pre-Lenten and Lenten seasons are taken from a book called the [Triodion](/wiki/Triodion "Triodion").

The weeks of the pre-Lenten Season break are:

* **[Zacchaeus](/wiki/Zacchaeus "Zacchaeus") Sunday** (Slavic tradition) is sometimes regarded as a pre-Lenten Sunday because of its place in the Slavic lectionary. In that tradition, it is the eleventh Sunday before [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter") (Easter). There are no hymns proper to this Sunday. Its only distinguishing feature is the reading of Luke 19:1-10, the Gospel concerning Zacchaeus. This [lectionary](/wiki/Lectionary "Lectionary") reading is sometimes also appointed on the same Sunday in the Byzantine ("Greek") lectionary, as well. The week following this Sunday is a normal, non-Lenten time, since it falls outside the Triodion.
* **The [Publican and the Pharisee](/wiki/Pharisee_and_the_Publican "Pharisee and the Publican")**: Tenth Sunday before Pascha (70 days). The week following this Sunday is a fast-free week, lest the faithful be tempted, like the [Pharisee](/wiki/Pharisee "Pharisee") to boast about fasting.
* **The [Prodigal Son](/wiki/Prodigal_Son "Prodigal Son")**: Ninth Sunday before Pascha (63 days). The week following this Sunday is the last during which the laity may eat meat or meat products. The fasting rules for this week are the same as those for non-Lenten periods.
* **The [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment")** or **Meat-Fare Sunday** (the last day meat may be eaten): Eighth Sunday before Pascha (56 days). The week following this Sunday is called [Cheese-Fare Week](/wiki/Cheese-Fare_Week "Cheese-Fare Week") and is a fast-free week, with the exception that meat and meat products are forbidden.
* **Sunday of Forgiveness** or **Cheese-Fare Sunday**: Seventh Sunday before Pascha (49 days). This Sunday is the last day dairy products may be consumed. Throughout Great Lent, fish, wine, and olive oil will be allowed only on certain days.
